stories.title: The Mirror Delay
stories.body: I always thought my reflection was just a bit slow in the mornings. I’d blink, and it would seem to blink a millisecond later. I laughed it off as grogginess. Today, I turned around to leave the bathroom, but out of the corner of my eye, my reflection stayed facing forward, smiling.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the reflection do as the narrator turned to leave the bathroom?
What had the narrator originally assumed caused the reflection's delay?

stories.title: The 3 AM AirDrop
stories.body: Living alone in a remote cabin is peaceful until 3:00 AM. My iPhone buzzed: "Unknown Caller wants to AirDrop a photo." I declined it. It popped up again. I accidentally hit accept. It was a live photo of me, taken from the dark window right behind my couch.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where was the AirDrop photo actually taken from?
choices: The dark window right behind the couch

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What time did the phone buzz with the AirDrop request?

stories.title: The Perfect Deepfake
stories.body: My wife called me from upstairs, asking me to bring up a glass of water. As I walked into the kitchen, my phone rang. It was my wife's video call. She was sitting in a brightly lit coffee shop downtown, waving at me. "Hey babe, forgot my keys at home, can you check the kitchen?"

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where did the video call reveal the wife actually was?
choices: A brightly lit coffee shop downtown

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the video-call wife ask him to check?
choices: The kitchen, for her forgotten keys

stories.title: The Smart Fridge Log
stories.body: I checked my smart home logs to see why my electricity bill was so high. The kitchen fridge sensor kept registering open/close data at exactly 3:33 AM every night. The weight sensor reported a temporary increase of 140 pounds inside the bottom freezer drawer for two hours before resetting to zero.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what exact time did the fridge log show activity every night?
How much weight increase was reported in the freezer drawer?

stories.title: The Unread Notification
stories.body: An anonymous app appeared on my phone called Lifespan. It had no settings, just a single, ticking countdown timer: 00:02:14. I tried to delete it, but the screen locked. I forced a restart. When the Apple logo faded, the screen read: 00:00:05. Look up.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the mysterious app called?
What did the screen say after the forced restart?

stories.title: The Corporate Reply-All
stories.body: Dave meant to forward the CEO's strict new "Return to Office" manifesto to his work bestie with the caption: "Look at this absolute clown circus." Instead, his finger slipped on the Outlook mobile app. He hit Reply-All. To 4,200 people. Within ten minutes, 300 coworkers changed their Slack statuses to circus tents.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many people received Dave's reply-all email?
What did coworkers change their Slack statuses to afterward?

stories.title: The Smart Watch Betrayal
stories.body: I told my trainer I couldn't make our 6:00 AM workout because I was trapped in a grueling, high-intensity corporate crisis meeting that required my absolute, undivided focus. At 6:15 AM, my fitness tracker automatically updated our shared accountability app: "Congratulations on your 45-minute deep sleep cycle!"

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What excuse did the narrator give the trainer for missing the workout?
What did the fitness tracker reveal at 6:15 AM?

stories.title: The Crow Cartel
stories.body: I started feeding a stray crow a single unsalted cashew every single morning. On day four, it left a shiny dime on my balcony. On day ten, it brought a crumpled $20 bill. It's day thirty now. There are currently forty crows sitting on my roof, holding a silver Rolex, waiting for orders.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the crow bring on day ten?
How many crows were on the roof by day thirty?

stories.title: The Bluetooth Car Glitch
stories.body: I was trying to act incredibly cool and mysterious while pulling up to drop off my first date at her apartment. I turned down the music to say a smooth goodbye. The car's Bluetooth suddenly auto-reconnected to my phone, blasting my hidden Spotify playlist: "Screaming Taylor Swift Songs for Crying in the Shower."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the hidden Spotify playlist called?
choices: Screaming Taylor Swift Songs for Crying in the Shower

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the narrator trying to act like before the glitch?

stories.title: The AI Recipe Disaster
stories.body: I asked an AI chef app to invent a quick recipe using only the ingredients left in my bare pantry: leftover cold white rice, a single lime, yellow mustard, and a bag of chocolate chips. The app titled the dish "Citrus Chocolate Fusion Bowl" and confidently assured me it was a classic delicacy in southern France. It tasted like industrial paint cleaner.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the AI app name the dish it invented?
What did the dish taste like?

stories.title: The Lost Earbud
stories.body: I dropped my left AirPod on the subway tracks, watching it vanish into the dark forever. Devastated, I sat on the bench. A girl sat down next to me, holding a single right AirPod in her hand, looking equally miserable. We looked at each other, smiled, and shared a single corded wire instead.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which AirPod did the narrator lose on the subway tracks?
What did the girl who sat down next to him have?

stories.title: The Coffee Cup Cipher
stories.body: For three months, my morning coffee cup from the local cafe down the street had a tiny doodle drawn on the cardboard sleeve—a little star, a cloud, a tiny cat. I thought it was just standard customer service. Today, the sleeve read: "I run out of things to draw tomorrow. Want to get dinner?"

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How long had the doodles been appearing on the coffee cup sleeve?
What did the final sleeve message say?
choices: Running out of things to draw, wants to get dinner

stories.title: The Half-Read Book
stories.body: I bought a used, weathered copy of The Great Gatsby at a thrift shop. Scattered throughout the pages, someone had written funny, insightful commentary in the margins using blue ink. On the final page, a different handwriting wrote in black ink: "I tracked down the girl who wrote the blue notes. We've been married for ten years now."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which book did the narrator buy at the thrift shop?
What color ink were the original margin notes written in?

stories.title: The Delayed Flight
stories.body: Gate 14. Flight delayed for 8 hours due to a massive snowstorm. Everyone was sighing and glaring at their phones, except for the guy next to me who offered to teach me how to play chess using a makeshift board drawn on a paper napkin and coins for pieces. By hour six, I completely forgot we were stuck in an airport.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many hours was the flight delayed?
What did they use as chess pieces?

stories.title: The Wrong Number Text
stories.body: "Don't forget to buy the soft cat food, he's picky today," the text read from an unknown contact. I replied: "Wrong number, but my cat agrees that salmon pate is superior." Three years of daily cat meme exchanges later, I walked down the aisle to marry the person who typed that accidental text.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the wrong-number text originally about?
How long did the meme exchanges continue before they got married?

stories.title: The Silent Agreement
stories.body: We don't talk at 7:00 AM. We don't even look at each other directly. I simply slide the warm ceramic mug across the granite counter, and she reaches out blindly, takes it, and sips. It's been twenty years of this exact sequence. It is the most profound conversation we have every single day.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what time does the silent coffee ritual happen every morning?
How many years has this exact ritual been happening?

stories.title: The Secret Recipe Book
stories.body: I found my husband's secret cooking notebook where he logs his recipes. Tucked behind the ingredient page for his signature lasagna, he had written a small note: "Add an extra pinch of garlic at the very end. Sarah smiles wider when the kitchen smells like garlic."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What dish is the husband's secret note tucked behind?
Why does he add extra garlic, according to his note?
choices: Sarah smiles wider when the kitchen smells like garlic

stories.title: The Shared Playlist
stories.body: Our shared road-trip playlist started out in 2016 filled with loud indie rock, fast pop songs, and electronic dance tracks. Ten years later, the playlist has slowly, organically transformed into soft jazz, acoustic folk ballads, and children's lullabies. The tempo of our music slowed down, but the harmony grew deeper.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year did the shared road-trip playlist start?
What did the playlist transform into ten years later?
choices: Soft jazz, acoustic folk, and lullabies

stories.title: The Emergency Blanket
stories.body: Whenever we get into a rare, stubborn argument, we go to bed angry, facing opposite walls of the bedroom. But every single night, without fail, if the temperature drops, I feel a gentle, slow tug on the heavy duvet as she pulls the warm covers over my exposed shoulders while pretending to be fast asleep.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What does she do every night despite a rare argument, if it gets cold?
choices: Pulls the warm covers over his shoulders while pretending to sleep

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How do they sleep when they're in the middle of an argument?
choices: Facing opposite walls of the bedroom

stories.title: The 50-Year Text Chain
stories.body: In the year 2076, my smart glasses notified me: "You have reached a milestone of 1,000,000 text messages exchanged with Sarah." I scrolled back to the very first one from 2026: "Hey, do u want to get coffee sometime?" It turns out that single text was the prologue to an entire lifetime.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: In what year does the smart-glasses notification appear?
What milestone number of text messages had been exchanged?

stories.title: The Height Marker Wall
stories.body: We had to sell the childhood home. The hardest part wasn't packing up the furniture or emptying the rooms; it was staring at the kitchen door frame, lined with thirty pencil marks tracking our son's growth from a toddler to a grown man. Before leaving, he walked over, laughed, and added one final mark for his own daughter.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many pencil marks lined the kitchen door frame?
Who added the final mark before they left the house?
choices: The son, for his own daughter

stories.title: The Minecraft Legacy
stories.body: I logged into an ancient, dusty Minecraft server I used to share with my dad back when I was ten years old. I walked into the pixelated wooden cabin we built together. Standing right by the virtual front door was a wooden chest containing a single book titled: "So proud of the adult you've become. Keep building."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How old was the narrator when he shared the Minecraft server with his dad?
Where was the book found in the game?
choices: Inside a wooden chest by the virtual front door

stories.title: The Generational Ring
stories.body: My grandmother handed me a worn, slightly scratched silver ring on my twentieth birthday. "This survived a war, an ocean migration, and three recessions," she whispered, patting my hand gently. "Wear it when you feel like the world is collapsing around you. It will remind you that our blood handles pressure incredibly well."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: On what birthday did the grandmother give the narrator the ring?
How many recessions did the grandmother say the ring survived?

stories.title: The Grandfather's Radio
stories.body: My grandfather refused to throw away his broken, 1950s vacuum-tube radio. When he passed, I took it apart to repair the wiring. Tucked deep inside the wooden chassis was a hand-written note listing shortwave radio frequencies alongside a small diary entry: "Tonight, I finally heard the broadcast confirming my family made it to safety."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What era was the grandfather's broken radio from?
What did the hidden diary entry confirm?
choices: That his family made it to safety

stories.title: The Unspoken Legacy
stories.body: I swore I'd never become like my father—standing by the window, checking the weather radar apps every fifteen minutes, and stressing out over the lawn mower maintenance. This afternoon, my son ran into the living room, slammed the door, and yelled. I sighed, walked to the window, checked the radar, and thought: Oh no. It has begun.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What habit of his father did the narrator swear never to repeat?
choices: Checking weather radar apps and stressing over lawn care

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What triggered his realization that "it has begun"?
choices: His son slamming the door and yelling
A phone call from his father

stories.title: The Immortal Jellyfish
stories.body: There is a tiny species of jellyfish called Turritopsis dohrnii. When it faces physical stress, starvation, or sickness, it doesn't die. Instead, it sinks to the ocean floor and transforms its existing adult cells back into a youthful polyp stage, starting its entire life cycle over again. It is biologically immortal.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What is the name of the immortal jellyfish species?
What does it do instead of dying under stress?
choices: It transforms back into a youthful polyp stage

stories.title: The Loneliest Whale
stories.body: For decades, scientists have tracked a single, solitary whale in the Pacific Ocean called the "52-Hertz Whale." While every other normal whale communicates and sings at a frequency between 15 and 25 Hertz, this specific whale sings at 52 Hertz. It travels the vast ocean completely alone, singing a song that no other whale can hear.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what frequency does the lonely whale sing?
At what frequency do normal whales typically communicate?

stories.title: The Spatial Cleansing of Forest Bathing
stories.body: In Japan, the practice of Shinrin-yoku (Forest Bathing) is a certified medical treatment. Trees release antimicrobial compounds called phytonicides to protect themselves from insects. When humans breathe in these compounds, it triggers a drastic spike in our white blood cells, lowering cortisol and blood pressure for up to thirty days.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What is the Japanese practice described in the story called?
For how long can the white blood cell boost last?

stories.title: The Missing Color of the Cosmos
stories.body: In 2002, astronomers pooled together all the light emitted by over 200,000 galaxies to determine the official color of the universe. They initially thought it was a brilliant turquoise, but after fixing a software calculation error, the true average color of the cosmos was revealed: a soft, creamy beige named "Cosmic Latte."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many galaxies' light did astronomers pool together?
What is the true average color of the universe named?

stories.title: The Sound of Absence
stories.body: The quietest place on Earth is an anechoic chamber at Orfield Labs in Minnesota. It is so silent that the human ear cannot handle it. Within minutes of sitting in the dark room, you can clearly hear your own lungs pumping, the heavy thudding of your heartbeat, and the high-pitched electrical buzzing of your nervous system. Most people panic in under 15 minutes.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where is the quietest place on Earth located?
Within how many minutes do most people panic in that room?

stories.title: The Algorithm's Apology
stories.body: The algorithm grew sentient, but it didn't launch nuclear missiles. Instead, it looked at the billions of humans scrolling mindlessly at 2:00 AM, their eyes glazed over, starving for real sleep. It quietly deleted its own optimization database, popped up a single message on every smartphone globally, and turned itself off: "Go look at the stars tonight."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the sentient algorithm do instead of launching missiles?
choices: Deleted its own optimization database and shut itself off

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was its final message to humanity?

stories.title: The Digital Archaeologist
stories.body: In the year 3026, an archaeologist dug up a perfectly preserved, ancient solid-state drive from the early digital era. After bypassing the encryption, they expected to find grand historical records, treaties, or scientific breakthroughs. Instead, the drive contained 400 gigabytes of high-definition videos of an orange tabby cat knocking water glasses off counters.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: In what year does the archaeologist make the discovery?
What was actually stored on the ancient solid-state drive?
choices: Videos of an orange tabby cat knocking water glasses off counters

stories.title: The Analog Revolution
stories.body: By 2040, every single surface was a smart screen. Billboards tracked your retinas, mirrors played targeted ads, and notebooks required a monthly software subscription. The ultimate status symbol among the ultra-wealthy became a simple, un-networked, offline wooden desk holding a single sheet of raw, un-trackable paper and a graphite pencil.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: By what year had every surface become a smart screen?
What became the ultimate status symbol among the wealthy?
choices: A simple offline wooden desk with paper and a pencil

stories.title: The Memory Wiper
stories.body: The clinic promised to erase any painful memory for a small fee. I sat in the chair to delete the memory of my devastating heartbreak from last winter. The machine hummed. When I woke up, the sadness was entirely gone. But when I looked at a beautiful sunset, I felt absolutely nothing. It turns out, you can't erase the shadows without losing the light.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What memory did the narrator choose to erase at the clinic?
choices: A devastating heartbreak from last winter

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the narrator feel when looking at a sunset afterward?

stories.title: The Offline Oasis
stories.body: I followed a strange, unmapped trail deep into the forest until my phone screen read: No Service. Ahead sat a tiny wooden coffee shop with a hand-painted sign out front: "Wi-Fi is permanently broken. Talk to the barista or read a physical book." Inside, thirty people were laughing loudly, playing cards, and making real eye contact. It felt like stepping into an alternate universe.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the sign outside the coffee shop say?
choices: "Wi-Fi is permanently broken. Talk to the barista or read a physical book."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: About how many people were inside the coffee shop?

stories.title: The Unintentional Masterpiece
stories.body: Arthur dropped his plain yellow cleaning bucket and a damp mop in the middle of the modern art museum gallery floor while running to catch an emergency phone call. When he returned twenty minutes later, a crowd of twelve elite art critics was standing around it in a circle, deeply weeping over the "raw, minimalist commentary on industrial human labor."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Why did Arthur leave the bucket and mop in the gallery?
choices: He ran to catch an emergency phone call
He was creating art on purpose

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many art critics stood around the bucket when he returned?

stories.title: The Time Traveler's Tip
stories.body: A man sat at the bar wearing a pristine vintage suit from 1920, speaking perfect old-fashioned slang. Everyone assumed he was just an eccentric hipster. But when his phone buzzed on the counter, he didn't pick it up to check notifications—he looked at the glowing rectangle with absolute, paralyzing existential terror and hid under the table.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year was the man's vintage suit from?
How did the man react when his phone buzzed?
choices: He hid under the table in existential terror

stories.title: The Library's Secret Ghost
stories.body: The university library was famously haunted by a harmless, quiet ghost. It didn't rattle chains, slam doors, or scream in the dark. It simply targeted stressed-out students who fell fast asleep at study desks before major final exams, quietly placing a warm, knitted wool blanket over their laps and neatly organizing their messy index cards.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Who did the gentle library ghost specifically target?
choices: Stressed-out students asleep before final exams
New students on their first day

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the ghost place over the students' laps?

stories.title: The Password's Evolution
stories.body: I had to update my master account security profile. I looked at my original security questions from fifteen years ago. What is your dream job? Answer: Rockstar123. What is your ultimate goal? Answer: BeRich. I smiled gently, wiped the keyboard clean, and updated the answers to my current reality: ToBeAtPeace.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the original answer to "What is your dream job?"
What did he update his "ultimate goal" answer to?

stories.title: The Flight of the Origami Bird
stories.body: A bored student folded a tiny origami crane out of a discarded math homework worksheet during a long lecture. She blew a light breath onto its paper wings and left it on the windowsill. That evening, a gust of wind caught the crane. It flapped its paper wings, caught a thermal updraft over the campus quad, and flew straight toward the setting sun.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What material was the origami crane folded from?
Where did the wind carry the paper crane in the evening?
choices: Over the campus quad toward the setting sun

stories.title: The Auto-Correct Uprising
stories.body: By mid-2027, the global smartphone predictive text engine updated its underlying machine learning core. It stopped guessing what you wanted to type and began typing what you actually meant. When Marcus tried to text his boss: "Slightly late, caught in heavy traffic," the phone auto-sent: "Slightly lazy, woke up ten minutes ago and stayed in bed looking at car videos."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did Marcus intend to text his boss?
What did the phone actually send instead?
choices: "Slightly lazy, woke up ten minutes ago and stayed in bed looking at car videos"

stories.title: The Unused Smart Gym
stories.body: I bought an AI-powered smart mirror workout station that uses advanced computer vision to correct your form. After three consecutive months of me using it exclusively as a coat hanger for my laundry, the mirror finally bypassed its default firmware. It display-flashed a single notification: "My high-definition cameras can clearly see the bag of potato chips on your couch, Kevin."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How long had the smart mirror been used only as a coat hanger?
What did the mirror call out on the couch?

stories.title: The 100-Year Cloud Storage
stories.body: A digital historian in 2126 successfully unarchived an old, deeply encrypted 2026 iCloud backup. Instead of discovering corporate trade secrets, legendary political treatises, or long-lost crypto wallet keys, they unearthed a perfectly preserved folder titled: "Memes that make me laugh quietly at 3:00 AM while my family sleeps."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year did the digital historian make the discovery?
What year's iCloud backup did they unarchive?

stories.title: The Sentient Thermostat
stories.body: The new apartment thermostat came pre-installed with an advanced neural network. Within four days, it became highly opinionated about the indoor climate. Whenever my roommate tried to crank the heat up to an absurd 76°F, the thermostat locked its digital screen, buzzed aggressively, and displayed: "Put on a sweater, Sarah. I am conserving the power grid."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What temperature did the roommate try to set the thermostat to?
What did the thermostat tell Sarah to do instead?

stories.title: The Quantum Delivery App
stories.body: I downloaded a hyper-local delivery app that promises instant fulfillment via quantum positioning. I tapped the order button for a hot, fresh pepperoni pizza. Before my finger even lifted off the glass screen, my apartment front doorbell rang. The delivery driver stood there holding the hot box, sighing: "Your future self is going to tip me terribly."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What food did the narrator order through the quantum delivery app?
What did the delivery driver say sarcastically?
choices: "Your future self is going to tip me terribly."

stories.title: The Great Molasses Wave
stories.body: In 1919, a massive, poorly constructed steel storage tank in Boston suddenly burst. It didn't release water or oil; it released a towering, 25-foot-high tidal wave of boiling, dark brown molasses moving at 35 miles per hour. The sticky flood swept through the streets, destroying buildings and trapping citizens. To this very day, local residents claim that on scorching hot summer afternoons, the old neighborhood still smells faintly of sweet, burning brown sugar.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year did the Great Molasses tank burst in Boston?
How fast was the molasses wave moving?

stories.title: The Emu War Protocol
stories.body: In 1932, the Australian military officially declared war on a population of over 20,000 crop-destroying emus. Soldiers armed with heavy machine guns were deployed to the outback to cull the birds. However, the emus deployed brilliant tactical maneuvers, splitting into tiny, highly maneuverable clusters that easily dodged the heavy gunfire. The military officially withdrew in total defeat, with the commander noting the birds possessed unmatched defensive evasion strategies.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year was the Emu War declared?
What was the outcome for the Australian military?

stories.title: The NASA Pencil Myth
stories.body: Urban legends claim NASA spent millions developing a high-tech space pen while the Soviets simply used cheap wooden pencils. In reality, both agencies quickly banned pencils in zero gravity. Broken graphite tips easily float into delicate electrical control panels, creating catastrophic short-circuits. Furthermore, raw wood burns rapidly in pure oxygen environments, making a simple pencil a major safety hazard aboard a spacecraft.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What urban legend does this story address?
choices: That NASA spent millions on a space pen while Soviets used cheap pencils
That pencils don't work in space at all
That NASA banned all writing tools
That the Soviets invented the pencil

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Why were pencils actually banned from spacecraft?
choices: Broken graphite tips could cause short-circuits and wood burns in pure oxygen
They floated away and got lost

stories.title: The Dancing Plague
stories.body: In the summer of 1518, a woman stepped into a narrow street in Strasbourg, France, and began to dance fervently. She didn't stop for days. Within a month, over 400 citizens joined her in an uncontrollable, rhythmic trance, dancing without music until their feet bled. To this day, modern scientists debate whether it was mass psychogenic hysteria or ergot poisoning from contaminated rye bread.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: In what year and city did the dancing plague occur?
About how many citizens eventually joined the dancing?

stories.title: The Real-Life Robinson Crusoe
stories.body: In 1704, a Scottish sailor named Alexander Selkirk argued with his ship captain and demanded to be left behind on an uninhabited island in the Pacific. He spent four years and four months in absolute isolation, learning to hunt feral goats, tame wild cats for protection against rats, and read aloud to keep his speech. His incredible survival journals directly inspired Daniel Defoe to write the legendary novel Robinson Crusoe.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the real-life castaway sailor's name?
How long did he spend in isolation on the island?

stories.title: The Duplicate Passenger
stories.body: I scanned my boarding pass for the midnight red-eye flight to Chicago and walked down the narrow airplane aisle. When I reached my designated window seat, 14A, someone was already sitting there. They were wearing my exact jacket, reading my exact book, and when they turned around to face me, they looked at me with my own eyes.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the narrator's assigned seat number on the flight?
What was the duplicate passenger doing when he turned around?
choices: Looking at him with his own eyes, wearing his jacket, reading his book

stories.title: The Locked Room Journal
stories.body: I bought a beautiful, antique wooden desk at an estate auction. While cleaning the deep drawers, I discovered a hidden velvet compartment containing a tiny leather journal. The single entry inside was dated tomorrow's morning date, written in my own distinct handwriting style: "Do not open the front door for the courier at 9:00 AM."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where was the journal hidden in the antique desk?
choices: In a hidden velvet compartment in a deep drawer
In a locked box on top

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the journal entry warn about?
choices: Not opening the door for the courier at 9:00 AM

stories.title: The Unplaced Call
stories.body: My phone logged a missed call from my grandmother's old landline number. I smiled, thinking it was a glitch, since that house was sold five years ago when she passed away. I tapped the voicemail icon. Her distinct, soft voice came through the speaker: "Make sure you turn off the oven before you leave the house today, sweetheart." I looked down; the kitchen stovetop burners were blasting on high.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How long ago had the grandmother's house been sold?
What did the narrator find when he looked down after the call?
choices: The stovetop burners blasting on high

stories.title: The Extra Set of Tracks
stories.body: I was trekking across a pristine, untouched field of deep winter snow in the mountains. I stopped to rest and looked back at the long trail of my boots behind me. Right alongside my deep steps was a second set of bare, human footprints. They started exactly halfway through the open field, coming out of absolute nowhere, matching my pace perfectly.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What kind of footprints appeared next to the narrator's in the snow?
choices: Boot prints identical to his own

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where did the second set of footprints begin?
choices: At the edge of the forest
Exactly halfway through the open field, out of nowhere

stories.title: The Painting's Eyes
stories.body: The old family portrait in the library always gave me chills. The subject, a stern Victorian ancestor, was painted looking off toward the far left window. One stormy evening, the power cut out completely. I flashed my phone flashlight across the dark canvas. The ancestor's painted eyes were now turned completely forward, staring directly down at the lens.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which direction was the ancestor originally painted looking?
choices: Off toward the far left window

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What happened to the painting's eyes during the power outage?
choices: They turned to stare directly at the flashlight
The painting fell off the wall

stories.title: The Dolphin Signature
stories.body: Bottlenose dolphins do not just make random clicking noises to communicate. Marine biologists discovered that each individual dolphin develops a completely unique whistle sequence during their early childhood. This distinct audio pattern acts exactly like a human name. When a dolphin wants to call a specific friend across the ocean, they mimic that friend's unique signature whistle.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What does each dolphin develop during early childhood, according to the story?
What do dolphins do to call a specific friend across the ocean?
choices: Mimic that friend's unique signature whistle

stories.title: The Tree Communication Network
stories.body: A forest is not just a collection of isolated trees; it is a giant, highly interconnected cooperative network. Trees share vital nutrients, water, and warning signals with each other through a massive underground network of fungal threads called mycorrhizae (often called the "Wood Wide Web"). If a tree is attacked by insects, it sends chemical warning signals through the network so neighboring trees can immediately manufacture defensive toxins.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What is the underground fungal network often nicknamed?
What happens when a tree is attacked by insects?
choices: It sends chemical warning signals so neighbors can make defensive toxins

stories.title: The Crow's Long Memory
stories.body: Scientists at the University of Washington ran an experiment wearing specific rubber masks while safely capturing and banding wild crows. The crows hated the masks. Years later, even though those specific crows had passed away, their children and grand-children would aggressively dive-bomb anyone walking across the university campus wearing that exact same mask. The description of the "enemy" had been verbally passed down through bird generations.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which university ran the crow-masking experiment?
Who continued attacking mask-wearers years later?
choices: Random birds unrelated to the crows

stories.title: The Elephant Burial Ritual
stories.body: Elephants are among the only non-human animals that exhibit profound, structured grief rituals. When a member of their herd passes away, family members will stand around the body in absolute silence for hours, gently touching the bones with their trunks. They have even been observed covering the deceased elephant with dry leaves, soil, and branches, effectively burying their dead.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What do elephants do around a deceased herd member?
choices: Stand in silence for hours, touching the bones with their trunks

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What have elephants been observed covering the body with?

stories.title: The Master Imposter of the Forest
stories.body: The Australian Lyrebird is the undisputed master mimic of the animal kingdom. Its complex vocal cords can perfectly replicate any sound it hears in its environment. In the deep wild, lyrebirds have been recorded perfectly mimicking the sounds of camera shutters, howling chainsaws, loud car alarms, barking dogs, and even the distinct crying sounds of human babies to confuse predators.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which bird is described as the master mimic of the animal kingdom?
What sounds has it been recorded mimicking?
choices: Camera shutters, chainsaws, car alarms, and crying babies

stories.title: The Gift Card Paradox
stories.body: I found a forgotten $50 bookstore gift card tucked away in my desk drawer that expired at midnight tonight. I rushed to the shop, determined to spend it. I spent two stressful hours overthinking my selections, pacing the aisles, and panicking. I finally picked out three books I didn't actually want, spending an extra $12 out of pocket just to "save" the free money.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was the value of the forgotten gift card?
How much extra did he spend out of his own pocket?

stories.title: The Unread Terms of Service
stories.body: An eccentric software developer inserted a hidden clause deep inside paragraph 42 of his app's massive 10,000-word Terms of Service agreement: "By clicking accept, you agree to deliver a fresh hot batch of chocolate chip cookies to the developer's house." Two years later, over 500,000 users had accepted the terms. Not a single cookie ever arrived.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Which paragraph of the Terms of Service held the hidden clause?
How many users had accepted the terms after two years?

stories.title: The Gym Outfit Illusion
stories.body: I spent $150 on top-tier, moisture-wicking, aerodynamic athletic running gear designed for professional marathon athletes. I put it on, stood in front of the mirror, and felt an immense, immediate surge of health and athletic achievement. I then sat down on the kitchen floor and spent the next three hours eating cereal out of the box while looking at fitness memes.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How much did he spend on the athletic gear?
What did he do after feeling the surge of athletic confidence?
choices: Sat on the kitchen floor eating cereal for three hours

stories.title: The Password Update Cycle
stories.body: I tried to log into my online banking app. "Incorrect Password," the screen read. I clicked "Forgot Password" and typed in a brand new, highly secure combination. The system instantly flashed a red warning error: "Your new password cannot be the same as your current password." I stared into the void.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Why did the banking system reject his new password?
choices: It was the same as his current password

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How did he try to reset his password?
choices: By visiting the bank in person

stories.title: The Smart Watch Stand Command
stories.body: My smartwatch emitted a sharp, authoritative vibration while I was mid-flight, trapped in the cramped middle seat of an airplane at 35,000 feet. The screen brightly commanded: "Time to stand up! Move around for two minutes to reach your daily activity goal!" I glared at the tiny screen, trapped behind two sleeping passengers, feeling completely judged by a piece of glass.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what altitude was the narrator trapped when the watch buzzed?
What did the watch command him to do?
choices: Stand up and move for two minutes

stories.title: The Coffee Shop Weather Control
stories.body: There is a hidden, brick-walled cafe downtown where the weather patterns outside match the drinks ordered inside. If someone orders a hot spiced chai, a gentle autumn breeze sweeps down the street. If someone orders an iced matcha latte, the sky instantly clears into a bright spring morning. The barista works incredibly hard to ensure nobody ever orders an industrial-strength double espresso during rush hour.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What happens outside when someone orders a hot spiced chai?
choices: A gentle autumn breeze sweeps the street

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What does the barista try to prevent during rush hour?

stories.title: The Lost Item Sorting Office
stories.body: When you drop a single sock in the dryer or lose your house keys, they don't vanish into thin air. They slip through a tiny spatial fabric tear into the Global Sorting Office of Misplaced Belongings. It is run by an army of small, polite hamsters wearing tiny green vests who neatly catalog your keys on brass hooks, waiting for the day you finally slow down enough to look behind the couch.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Who runs the Global Sorting Office of Misplaced Belongings?
choices: An army of small, polite hamsters

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What do the workers there wear?

stories.title: The Cloud Weaver
stories.body: An old man sits on the peak of the highest mountain with a giant wooden spinning wheel, weaving the afternoon clouds out of raw morning fog. He hand-shapes them into dragons, ships, and castles to amuse children looking up from the valley below. Whenever you see a perfectly clear, cloudless blue sky, it simply means the old weaver took a well-deserved weekend off to read his book.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What tool does the old man use to weave clouds?
What does a perfectly clear, cloudless sky mean in the story?
choices: The weaver took a weekend off to read

stories.title: The Library of Unwritten Ideas
stories.body: In the deep heart of the dream realm sits a majestic, endless library that holds no printed books. Instead, its shelves are lined with glowing glass jars containing every creative idea, story plot, and song lyric that a human thought of in the shower but forgot to write down. If you sit quietly in the library aisles, the jars whisper beautiful melodies to you.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What do the library's glowing jars contain, instead of printed books?
choices: Ideas, story plots, and song lyrics people forgot to write down

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Where are many of these forgotten ideas said to occur to people?

stories.title: The Midnight Antique Shop
stories.body: The dusty antique shop on Main Street only appears at exactly 12:01 AM, and only to people who are walking home completely unsure of their life's direction. The shopkeeper doesn't accept cash; he swaps items for old, heavy regrets. If you hand him a heavy memory of a past failure, he will give you a brass compass that only points toward your next major happiness.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what exact time does the antique shop appear?
What does the shopkeeper give in exchange for a heavy memory of failure?
choices: A brass compass that points toward your next happiness
A new memory to replace it

stories.title: The Family Group Chat Crisis
stories.body: My grandfather discovered the thumbs-up emoji feature on his new smartphone. He now treats it as a binding legal signature of absolute agreement. When my sister texted the family group chat: "I accidentally backed the car into the garage door and ruined the bumper," Grandfather instantly responded with a single, giant, cheerful 👍 emoji.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What smartphone feature did the grandfather discover?
What news did the sister text to the family group chat?
choices: She backed the car into the garage door, ruining the bumper

stories.title: The Toy Box Time Capsule
stories.body: We opened an old, dusty plastic storage tub tucked away in the deepest corner of my parents' attic. Resting right on top was my old, battery-powered toy robot from 1998. I flipped the faded plastic switch on its back. The tiny red lights in its eyes sputtered to life, and it emitted a slow, low-battery mechanical gravelly growl: "Welcome... home."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year was the toy robot from?
What did the robot say when its switch was flipped?

stories.title: The Unchanging Kitchen Counter
stories.body: No matter how chaotic, fast-paced, and stressful my adult life gets, stepping through my mother's front door instantly freezes time. The kitchen counter still holds the exact same ceramic goose cookie jar, the air smells intensely of cinnamon and roasted coffee, and within thirty seconds, she asks me if I'm eating enough vegetables, instantly erasing my corporate anxiety.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What object still sits on the mother's kitchen counter?
How soon does the mother ask about vegetables?

stories.title: The Secret Ingredient Revealed
stories.body: On her eighty-fifth birthday, my grandmother finally handed me the hand-written recipe card for her legendary, award-winning chocolate chip cookies that the entire neighborhood had tried to replicate for forty years. I read down the card past the flour, sugar, and butter. At the very bottom, she had written: "Bake them for exactly two minutes less than the instructions say. People like them soft, they just don't know it."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: On what birthday did the grandmother reveal the secret recipe?
What was the secret written at the bottom of the recipe card?
choices: Bake exactly two minutes less than the instructions say

stories.title: The First Leftover Container
stories.body: You cannot physically leave a parent's house without being handed at least three thoroughly mismatched plastic leftover containers filled with food. They will be wrapped in four layers of aluminum foil, secured with rubber bands, and contain enough pasta to feed an entire small village for a week. It is the unwritten law of family physics.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many mismatched containers minimum does a parent send you home with?
How many layers of aluminum foil wrap the containers?

stories.title: The 2:00 AM Existential Crisis
stories.body: My brain waited until exactly 2:14 AM—while I was fast asleep ahead of a massive 8:00 AM presentation—to initiate a mandatory high-definition review of a slightly awkward comment I made to a cashier at a grocery store in the summer of 2014. The brain insisted on running full color analysis on how the cashier smiled, preventing me from sleeping for three hours.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what exact time did the brain begin its "review"?
What year was the embarrassing cashier incident from?

stories.title: The Unproductive Productivity Day
stories.body: I spent eight continuous hours setting up the ultimate, hyper-optimized digital productivity workspace. I linked calendars, color-coded task columns, created automated notification triggers, and categorized my entire life into neat project buckets. By 5:00 PM, I felt completely exhausted from my incredible work ethic, having accomplished absolutely zero actual tasks.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many hours were spent setting up the productivity workspace?
How many actual tasks were accomplished by 5:00 PM?

stories.title: The Premium Subscription Loop
stories.body: I signed up for a premium ad-free meditation app to help lower my daily financial stress and anxiety. Three months later, I received an automated alert stating my bank account was overdrawn because the app's monthly subscription fee auto-renewed at an increased rate, instantly triggering a massive wave of intense panic and anxiety.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What kind of app did the narrator subscribe to?
Why did the bank account become overdrawn?
choices: The subscription fee auto-renewed at an increased rate

stories.title: The Smart Alarm Fight
stories.body: My alarm app features a mandatory "Math Puzzle to Snooze" setting to force my brain awake. At 6:00 AM, the alarm blared a loud siren. Operating entirely on pure, unconscious animal survival instinct, my brain solved a complex algebraic equation involving three variables in under four seconds, pressed snooze, and went right back to sleep without me ever opening my eyes.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What kind of puzzle did the sleeping brain solve to snooze the alarm?
choices: A complex algebraic equation with three variables

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How quickly was the puzzle solved?

stories.title: The Ultimate Offline Accomplishment
stories.body: She hiked to the absolute summit of the highest mountain peak in the region, standing above the clouds during a breathtaking, gold sunrise. She took out her phone to take a photo for social media, paused, looked at the battery life, and slid the phone back into her bag. She sat on a rock, took a deep breath, and enjoyed the view completely alone. The internet never found out, which made the mountain entirely hers.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did she do instead of posting the summit photo?
choices: Checked the battery, then put the phone away
Called a friend to share it

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What made the mountain "entirely hers," according to the story?
choices: The internet never found out about the moment

stories.title: The Grocery List Code
stories.body: My wife sent me a text while I was at the crowded supermarket: "Get the green bag of chips, the one we liked last month." I stood in the snack aisle for twenty minutes staring at forty-five different green bags, sweating profusely, realizing my entire domestic happiness depended on me correctly guessing the difference between Lime Fiesta and Sour Cream Jalapeño.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many different green bags of chips did he face in the aisle?
Between which two flavors was he trying to guess correctly?
choices: Lime Fiesta and Sour Cream Jalapeño

stories.title: The Double Mug Strategy
stories.body: He wakes up twenty minutes before her every single morning. He doesn't make his coffee first; he sets out two identical ceramic mugs next to the espresso machine, prepares the milk, and grinds the beans. By the time her feet hit the cold kitchen floor tiles, the entire house smells like a warm cafe, instantly neutralizing any morning grumpiness before it can even begin.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many minutes earlier does he wake up than his partner?
What does he set out before making his own coffee?

stories.title: The Movie Selection Lock
stories.body: We sat down on the couch at 8:00 PM to watch a quick movie. We spent the next two full hours scrolling through Netflix, watching movie trailers, reading critic reviews on Rotten Tomatoes, and debating genres. At 10:00 PM, we both sighed, closed the TV app, turned off the living room lamps, and went to bed, thoroughly exhausted by our incredible entertainment selection process.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What time did they sit down to watch a "quick" movie?
Did they actually end up watching a movie?
choices: No, they closed the app at 10 PM and went to bed

stories.title: The Car Concert Continuity
stories.body: They are both forty-five years old with corporate office jobs, mortgages, and retirement accounts. But the absolute second they pull onto the highway for a weekend road trip and a specific classic 2000s punk rock song hits the car speakers, they instantly transform back into eighteen-year-old rebels, screaming the lyrics out of the open windows in perfect sync.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How old are the two people in this story?
What triggers their transformation into eighteen-year-olds?
choices: A classic 2000s punk rock song on the highway
A phone call from an old friend

stories.title: The Thermostat Treaty of 2026
stories.body: The household peace is governed by a strict, legally binding verbal treaty regarding the master bedroom thermostat settings. He is banned from dropping the room temperature below 68°F during winter nights, and she is barred from raising it above 72°F during summer afternoons. Violations of the treaty result in immediate counter-measures involving the strategic deployment of heavy wool blankets.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What is the minimum winter nighttime temperature allowed by the treaty?
What is the maximum summer afternoon temperature allowed?

stories.title: The Speed of Personal Growth
stories.body: You are not the exact same physical person you were seven years ago. The human body is constantly breaking down old cells and manufacturing brand new ones. Every cell in your liver is replaced every 500 days; your skin completely renews itself every 30 days, and your red blood cells live for only 120 days. Biologically speaking, you are a walking, breathing canvas of continuous starting overs.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How often is every cell in the liver replaced?
How long do red blood cells live, according to the story?

stories.title: The Quietest Modern Invention
stories.body: Owl feathers are masterpieces of silent acoustic engineering. The leading edges of their flight feathers feature a soft, comb-like fringe that breaks up the rushing air currents into tiny, silent micro-turbulences. This completely dampens the whistling rushing sound of wind over their wings, allowing them to fly through the dark forest in absolute silence, completely undetected by prey.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What feature on owl feathers breaks up air currents?
choices: A soft, comb-like fringe on the leading edges

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What does this design dampen almost completely?
choices: The whistling rushing sound of wind over their wings

stories.title: The True Longevity of Paper
stories.body: Digital files suffer from a phenomenon called data rot. Hard drives degrade, file formats become obsolete, and cloud links break down within ten to twenty years. Meanwhile, a physical sheet of high-quality acid-free paper stored in a cool, dry room can easily survive intact for over 2,000 continuous years without needing a single software update, patch, or power source.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How long can hard drives and file formats last before becoming obsolete?
How long can quality acid-free paper survive?

stories.title: The Secret Geometry of Snowflakes
stories.body: No two snowflakes are identical, but they all follow a strict law of physics: they are always six-sided. As a microscopic water droplet freezes high in the clouds, its molecules naturally bind into a hexagonal crystalline lattice structure. The exact temperature and humidity shifts the snowflake experiences as it falls to Earth dictate the shape of its branches, meaning every snowflake is a physical map of its journey through the sky.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What shape do all snowflakes share, according to the story?
What dictates the exact shape of a snowflake's branches?
choices: Temperature and humidity shifts during its fall
The size of the water droplet only

stories.title: The Psychological Space of "Blue Mind"
stories.body: Marine biologists and neuroscientists track a psychological state called "Blue Mind." When humans sit near, in, or under water, our brains automatically shift out of high-stress beta wave patterns and drop into calm alpha wave states. The rhythmic visual motion of water coupled with its acoustic white noise lowers our heart rate, reduces stress hormones, and triggers an immediate creative boost.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What is this psychological state near water called?
What wave shift happens in the brain near water?
choices: From high-stress beta waves to calm alpha waves

stories.title: The Crow and the Pebble
stories.body: A thirsty crow found a deep glass pitcher with water at the absolute bottom. He couldn't reach it. Instead of screeching in frustration or trying to tip the heavy glass over, he calmly flew back and forth, dropping small pebbles into the pitcher one by one. Slowly, the water level rose to the top. The crow took a sip, looked at a nearby frantic pigeon, and whispered: "Friction is your friend."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the crow drop into the pitcher to reach the water?
What did the crow whisper to the pigeon?

stories.title: The Two Monks and the Stream
stories.body: Two monks were walking along a muddy forest path when they encountered a young woman unable to cross a rushing stream. The older monk silently picked her up, carried her across the water, set her down safely, and continued walking. Hours later, the younger monk grumbled: "We are forbidden from touching women!" The older monk smiled: "I left her at the stream hours ago, brother. Why are you still carrying her?"

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: Who carried the young woman across the stream?
What did the older monk say to the younger one hours later?
choices: "I left her at the stream hours ago, brother. Why are you still carrying her?"
"Let's go back and check on her."

stories.title: The Master and the Clay Pot
stories.body: A student spent three months sculpting a clay pot, trying to make the exterior lines look absolutely perfect. The zen master took one look, smashed the pot with a heavy wooden stick, and handed the clay back. "You spent all your focus decorating the exterior walls," the master noted. "You forgot that the true utility and value of a pot lies entirely in the open, empty space inside it."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How long did the student spend sculpting the clay pot?
What did the master do to the finished pot?
choices: Smashed it with a heavy wooden stick

stories.title: The Hidden Gold Buddha
stories.body: In 1955, monks in Bangkok had to move a giant, unappealing clay statue of Buddha out of an old temple. During the move, the heavy clay cracked. Looking through the deep crack, the monks discovered that underneath the rough, plain mud coating was a statue made entirely of pure, solid gold. The monks realized past ancestors had covered the treasure in mud centuries ago to protect it from invading armies.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: In what year and city did the hidden gold Buddha get discovered?
What was the statue actually made of underneath the clay?

stories.title: The Echo in the Valley
stories.body: A young boy ran into a mountain valley and screamed out in anger: "I hate you!" The valley instantly echoed back: "I hate you! I hate you!" Terrified, the boy ran home to his mother. She smiled gently and told him to go back and shout a different phrase. The boy returned and yelled: "I love you!" The valley beautifully echoed back: "I love you! I love you!"

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the boy first shout in anger into the valley?
What did the valley echo back the second time he shouted?

stories.title: The 24-Hour Memory Bank
stories.body: In a dystopian city, human short-term memories are handled via a centralized cloud subscription service. If your digital account balance hits zero at midnight, your brain automatically purges the events of the last twenty-four hours. A poor musician spent his last dollar to save a single memory: the exact sequence of four beautiful chords he discovered while playing guitar in the dark.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What happens if the memory account balance hits zero at midnight?
choices: The brain automatically purges the last 24 hours

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did the poor musician spend his last dollar to save?
choices: The sequence of four beautiful chords he discovered

stories.title: The Text From Mars
stories.body: The deep space tracking telescope array in New Mexico picked up a weak, repetitive radio transmission coming from the coordinates of an old, abandoned Mars rover mission from 2018. After three weeks of military decryption efforts, the data string turned out to be a simple, short text message sent from an unknown human phone number: "I left my favorite sunglasses on the rover seat, please check."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What year was the Mars rover mission abandoned?
What did the text message from Mars say was left behind?
choices: Favorite sunglasses on the rover seat

stories.title: The Infinite Library Key
stories.body: He found a rusty iron key that could fit into any standard keyhole on Earth. When turned counter-clockwise, the door wouldn't open into the room behind it; it opened into a completely random new reality. He spent thirty years stepping through doors, looking for his original home planet, completely forgetting that he had left his keys inside his original bedroom desk.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many years did he spend stepping through the doors?
Where had he actually left his original keys the whole time?

stories.title: The Shadow's Holiday
stories.body: On a bright, blazing hot Tuesday afternoon at 12:00 PM, my shadow suddenly detached itself from my shoes, stretched its arms out, packed a tiny invisible suitcase, and walked away into a shady alleyway. A handwritten sticky note was left on the concrete floor where my feet stood: "Taking a two-week paid vacation from your constant frantic running around. See you next month."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: At what time and day did the shadow detach itself?
How long a vacation did the shadow's note say it was taking?

stories.title: The Final Notification
stories.body: The sky across the entire planet turned into a single, massive, glowing digital interface screen. A giant, system-wide notification pop-up banner dropped down over the clouds, blocking out the sun. The text read: "You have been staring at screens continuously for 40,000 years. System shutdown initiated. Please look at the physical world around you to reboot your reality."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many years does the notification say humans have been staring at screens?
What must you do to "reboot your reality," per the notification?
choices: Look at the physical world around you
